retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated P. vivax infections in chloroquine-resistant regions (Indonesia, Papua New Guinea) get treated with quinine sulfate as per above plus either doxycycline, primaquine, or tafenoquine as per above; or atovaquone-proguanil as per above plus either primaquine or tafenoquine; or mefloquine as per above plus either primaquine or tafenoquine as per above.

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria Prophylaxis -- Function -- Chemoprophylaxis Choices
retrieved_docs.content: Tafenoquine: Tafenoquine is active against hypnotize and erythrocytic forms of Plasmodium species. It also has activity against pre-erythrocytic forms, which prevents relapses of P. vivax. It is approved for prophylaxis for those over 16 years old. Dosing is daily for three days before travel to an endemic area, then weekly until seven days after return from travel. This drug also may cause life-threatening hemolytic anemia in patients with G6PD deficiency, and testing is essential before initiating the regimen. There are reports of psychiatric side effects, and use should discontinue in cases of psychotic symptoms.  Other side effects include GI upset, headache, asymptomatic elevations in methemoglobin levels and epithelial keratopathy. Because the medication has a half-life of 17 days, delayed presentations of adverse reactions may occur. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Tafamidis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the mechanism of action of tafamidis. Review the available formulations and dosage of tafamidis. Identify the adverse events of tafamidis in clinical practice. Outline the importance of collaboration and coordination in an interprofessional health team to improve patient outcomes when receiving tafamidis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Uncomplicated P. vivax or P. ovale infections in chloroquine-sensitive regions receive treatment with chloroquine phosphate or hydroxychloroquine as per above, plus either primaquine phosphate 30 mg (pediatric: 0.5 mg/kg) daily for 14 days or tafenoquine 300 mg once (same in children older than 16 years).

retrieved_docs.title: Malaria Prophylaxis -- Issues of Concern -- Special Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding: There is not enough antimalarial drug excreted in breastmilk to provide chemoprophylaxis to infants. As mefloquine, chloroquine, and hydroxychloroquine are safe for infants; they are also reasonable during breastfeeding. Little data exists for doxycycline, but it is generally not recommended for use in the breastfeeding mother. Atovaquone-proguanil also has little safety data and is not recommended for prophylaxis in those breastfeeding infants under 5 kg. Primaquine also has no available data; however, both the mother and the infant require G6PD deficiency testing before prophylaxis with primaquine. Tafenoquine is also not recommended due to a lack of data.

retrieved_docs.title: Deadly Single Dose Agents -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antimalarials - chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroquine and Hydroxychloroquine Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine are 4-aminoquinoline compounds that are commonly used in the prevention and treatment of malaria. Hydroxychloroquine is also frequently used in the treatment of rheumatologic diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus and gained notoriety during the COVID-19 pandemic when it was used experimentally in the treatment of SARS-CoV-2 infection. As the use of these drugs is common and spans many indications, the interprofessional healthcare team should be aware of potential drug toxicities and how to manage them.  This activity reviews the indications for use, mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetics, potential toxicities, and management of toxicities related to chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine.  Furthermore, this activity will empower members of the interprofessional healthcare team to recognize, treat, and mitigate chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine toxicity in the patients they serve.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumid Lupus Erythematosus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with limited TLE refractory to topical therapy and patients with extensive TLE should be treated with first line antimalarial therapy. Hydroxychloroquine or chloroquine can be used initially. Hydroxychloroquine is usually well tolerated than chloroquine. Between 200 and 400 mg per day of hydroxychloroquine is a reasonable dose. For chloroquine, a dose of 125 to 250 mg per day (for 5 to 7 days per week) is commonly used. Hydroxychloroquine and chloroquine may not be used in combination due to the risk of retinal toxicity. Their risk of retinal toxicity limits the maximal dose of these antimalarials. It is important to note that it may take 8 to 12 weeks to appreciate the effects of antimalarials. [18] Patients should have an eye exam at baseline and then periodically while on the antimalarials. Patients need to be aware of the skin depigmentation that can occur with both hydroxychloroquine and chloroquine. This presents as a blue-gray discoloration on the shins, palate, nails, or face, and this discoloration may be permanent. Gastrointestinal side effects are the most common reason for discontinuation of antimalarial treatment. Neuromuscular and hematological side effects are also noted.

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroquine and Hydroxychloroquine Toxicity -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: To reduce the incidence of chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine chronic toxicity, healthcare providers that prescribe these medications and those that screen for ocular toxicity have developed clear dosing and screening protocols that have reduced the risk of end-stage disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Antimalarial Medications -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Primaquine phosphate is utilized as an add-on agent to either chloroquine phosphate or hydroxychloroquine when infections are caused by P. vivax or P. ovale with chloroquine sensitivity. This medication works by eliminating the hypnozoites that remain dormant in the patient’s liver, which reduces the risk of patient relapse. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroquine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The patient informs their primary care provider about plans to travel to a country with malaria prevalence. Similarly, a primary care provider may also ask the patient during a normal history and physical whether the patient intends to travel outside of the country they reside.

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroquine and Hydroxychloroquine Toxicity -- Evaluation -- Interpretation of Results
retrieved_docs.content: mf-ERG: amplitude reduction is most common in ring 2, followed by rings 3, 4, and 1. Delayed implicit times are less common. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroquine and Hydroxychloroquine Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of retinal toxicity with chronic use of hydroxychloroquine was found to be 0.38% in a 2003 study of 526 patients and as high as 0.68% in a study conducted in 2010 in which the subjects used the medication for 5 to 7 years. [9] Based on this study, the American Academy of Ophthalmology recommended new screening guidelines in 2011. The same study revealed that the most important predictor of chronic ocular toxicity was the duration of use (cumulative dose), age, the daily dose, and patient weight did not correlate significantly with hydroxychloroquine toxicity. Another study revealed a much higher overall risk of 7.5% retinal toxicity among the 2361 patients studied. [10] This study also found that kidney disease and tamoxifen therapy increased the risk of retinopathy. A report in 2015 described that pericentral maculopathy without the classic parafoveal retinopathy was more common in Asian patients (50%) compared to Caucasian patients (2%), and Asian patients should receive adapted screening tests such as wider angle threshold visual field tests. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Chloroquine and Hydroxychloroquine Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the treatment indications for chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine. Determine the mechanisms of action of chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine. Identify the presence of chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine toxicities in patients. Collaborate with the interprofessional healthcare team to implement appropriate management strategies in patients with chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 29
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial therapy, which is largely delivered in oral tablet form. The antimalarial class includes a wide range of agents such as acedapsone; artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg tablets; atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg tablets and the lower‑strength atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg tablets that combine a mitochondrial electron‑transport inhibitor with a dihydrofolate‑reductase antagonist; chloroquine phosphate tablets (250 mg and 500 mg); hydroxychloroquine sulfate tablets (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg), both 4‑aminoquinoline compounds active against blood‑stage parasites; mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg tablets; primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg tablets targeting hepatic stages; pyrimethamine 25 mg tablets; and tafenoquine tablets (100 mg and 150 mg) that interfere with folate metabolism or mitochondrial function. Additional substances mentioned in the source data

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 498
retrieved_docs.content: The medications atovaquone 62.5 mg/proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg, griseofulvin 500 mg, hydrocortisone 5 mg, hydrocortisone 10 mg, hydrocortisone 20 mg, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg, pyrimethamine 25 mg, tafenoquine 100 mg, and tafenoquine 150 mg are all formulated as oral tablets. This uniform tablet dose form facilitates oral administration, standardizes prescribing practices, and supports patient adherence across a range of therapeutic classes, including antimalarials, antifungals, corticosteroids, antiprotozoal agents, and antiretroviral drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 923
retrieved_docs.content: Artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg Oral Tablet, atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg Oral Tablet, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg Oral Tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 10 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 5 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 300 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg Oral Tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg Oral Tablet, tafenoquine 100 mg Oral Tablet, and tafenoquine 150 mg Oral Tablet are all formulated for oral administration in tablet form. These tablets encompass a range of therapeutic classes—including antimalarial agents (artemether/lumefantrine, atovaquone/proguanil, chloroquine, mefloquine, tafenoquine), an antimalarial/antirheumatic agent (hydroxychloroquine), a corticosteroid (hydrocortisone), and an antifungal (griseofulvin)—but share the common dosage form of a solid tablet, facilitating standardized prescribing, dispensing, and patient adherence across these diverse medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 894
retrieved_docs.content: Atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg Oral Tablet, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg Oral Tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 5 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 10 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 20 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg Oral Tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg Oral Tablet, pyrimethamine 25 mg Oral Tablet, and tafenoquine 100 mg Oral Tablet are all formulated as oral tablets. These agents span several therapeutic classes—antimalarials (atovaquone/proguanil, chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, mefloquine, pyrimethamine, tafenoquine), an antifungal (griseofulvin), and a corticosteroid (hydrocortisone)—yet they share the same dose‑form, facilitating standardized prescribing, dispensing, and patient administration across diverse treatment indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 443
retrieved_docs.content: Atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg oral tablet, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg oral tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg oral tablet, hydrocortisone oral tablets in 5 mg, 10 mg and 20 mg strengths, hydroxychloroquine sulfate oral tablets in 300 mg and 400 mg strengths, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet, and tafenoquine oral tablets in 100 mg and 150 mg strengths are all formulated as solid oral dosage forms commonly referred to as tablets. Recognizing that each of these medications is delivered via the tablet dose form is important for prescribing practices, ensuring appropriate patient counseling on administration, and anticipating pharmacokinetic characteristics associated with oral solid dosage forms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 514
retrieved_docs.content: Antimalarials constitute a therapeutic class used to treat infections caused by Plasmodium parasites, the causative agents of malaria. Within this class, a variety of specific agents are recognized, including acedapson, the fixed‑dose combination artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablet, chinidin, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablet, and hydroxychloroquine sulfate tablets available in 100 mg, 200 mg, and 400 mg strengths. Additional antimalarial medications comprise mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine 100 mg oral tablet. Each of these drugs possesses distinct pharmacologic properties and dosing regimens, yet all share the common purpose of eliminating Plasmodium species from the patient. Awareness of the full spectrum of antimalarial agents supports clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy based on parasite species, resistance patterns, and individual patient considerations, thereby optimizing malaria management and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 300
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a broad class of antimalarial agents. This antimalarial class encompasses a variety of drugs, each with specific formulations and therapeutic roles. Acedapson is identified as an antimalarial, as are chinidin, chloroquine phosphate (500 mg oral tablet), hydroxychloroquine sulfate (300 mg oral tablet), mefloquine hydrochloride (250 mg oral tablet), mirincamycin, primaquine phosphate (26.3 mg oral tablet), pyrimethamine (25 mg oral tablet), sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine (150 mg oral tablet). Together, these medications provide the pharmacologic options for preventing and treating Plasmodium infections, with drug selection guided by the specific Plasmodium species, resistance patterns, and individual patient considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 662
retrieved_docs.content: Artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg Oral Tablet, atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg Oral Tablet, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg Oral Tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 5 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 10 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 20 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg Oral Tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg Oral Tablet, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg Oral Tablet, tafenoquine 100 mg Oral Tablet, and tafenoquine 150 mg Oral Tablet are all formulated as tablets for oral administration. This shared dosage form indicates that each of these medications is intended to be swallowed whole, providing a convenient and standardized route of delivery for a range of therapeutic classes, including antimalarials, antifungals, corticosteroids, and antirheumatic agents. Recognizing that these diverse agents share the tablet dosage form assists clinicians in prescribing, counseling patients on proper ingestion, and managing medication inventories across various treatment protocols.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 507
retrieved_docs.content: Artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg Oral Tablet, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg Oral Tablet, griseofulvin 500 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 5 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 10 mg Oral Tablet, hydrocortisone 20 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 300 mg Oral Tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 400 mg Oral Tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg Oral Tablet, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg Oral Tablet, and tafenoquine 150 mg Oral Tablet are all pharmaceutical products whose dosage form is a tablet. The relationship “has_dose_form” consistently links each of these drug entities to the tablet dosage form, indicating that they are manufactured and administered as oral tablets. These tablets encompass a range of therapeutic classes: artemether/lumefantrine, chloroquine phosphate, mefloquine hydrochloride, primaquine phosphate, and tafenoquine are antimalarial agents; hydroxychloroquine sulfate is used for malaria and autoimmune conditions; griseofulvin is an antifungal; and hydrocortisone tablets provide systemic corticosteroid therapy. Recognizing that all of these medications share the same tablet dosage form aids clinicians in prescribing, dispensing, and counseling patients about the route of administration and handling of these diverse therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is treated with a diverse group of medications collectively referred to as antimalarials. The antimalarial class encompasses a wide range of agents, including acedapson, the fixed‑dose combination artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablet, chinidin, chinin, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablet, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg oral tablet, mefloquine hydrochloride 250 mg oral tablet, mirincamycin, pyrimethamine 25 mg oral tablet, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine 150 mg oral tablet. Each of these drugs is recognized as an antimalarial and is employed to prevent or cure Plasmodium infection through distinct pharmacologic mechanisms. Together, they form the therapeutic arsenal essential for effective malaria management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2234
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of a diverse group of antimalarial agents. Among these agents, acedapson is classified as an antimalarial, as are chinidin and hydroxychloroquine sulfate formulated as a 100 mg oral tablet. The antimalarial category also includes the combination therapy of trimethoprim and sulfamethoxazole, mefloquine hydrochloride provided as a 250 mg oral tablet, mirincamycin, pyrimethamine supplied as a 25 mg oral tablet, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine available in both 100 mg and 150 mg oral tablet strengths. Collectively, these medications represent the pharmacologic options used to prevent and treat Plasmodium‑induced malaria, highlighting the breadth of therapeutic strategies employed in malaria management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 893
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is the primary target of antimalarial agents. Antimalarials constitute a therapeutic class that includes a variety of oral tablet formulations used to treat or prevent Plasmodium infection. Acedapson is classified as an antimalarial, and the antimalarial class further comprises artemether 20 mg / lumefantrine 120 mg oral tablets, atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg oral tablets, chinidin, chloroquine phosphate 500 mg oral tablets, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 100 mg oral tablets, hydroxychloroquine sulfate 300 mg oral tablets, primaquine phosphate 26.3 mg oral tablets, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine 150 mg oral tablets. Each of these agents works through distinct mechanisms to inhibit the growth, replication, or survival of Plasmodium species, thereby providing clinicians with multiple options for effective malaria management and prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: Plasmodium, the protozoan parasite that causes malaria, is directly linked to the therapeutic class of antimalarials, which are employed to eradicate or control infection. Within this class, acedapson is identified as an antimalarial agent. Several specific antimalarial formulations are also recognized as members of this group, including the combination tablets atovaquone 250 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 100 mg and atovaquone 62.5 mg / proguanil hydrochloride 25 mg, the quinoline derivative chinidin, chloroquine phosphate 250 mg oral tablet, and two dosage strengths of hydroxychloroquine sulfate (200 mg and 400 mg oral tablets). The antimalarial class further encompasses the agents mirincamycin, sulfadoxin, and tafenoquine 150 mg oral tablet, each classified as an antimalarial. Collectively, these entities illustrate the breadth of pharmacologic options available for targeting Plasmodium species and underscore the importance of selecting appropriate antimalarial agents based on drug composition and dosing considerations.
